[[BLOCK:slide0_shape0]]
Limitations of OLS Regression

[[BLOCK:slide0_shape1]]
Chong Ho Yu

[[BLOCK:slide1_shape0]]
Historical Significance of OLS Regression

[[BLOCK:slide1_shape1]]
Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression, also referred to as Standard Least Squares (SLS) regression, was developed by Legendre (1805) and Gauss (1809), dating back to the era of our great grandparents.

[[BLOCK:slide2_shape0]]
Understanding OLS Regression

[[BLOCK:slide2_shape1]]
Least squares = minimizing the square of residuals  
Residual (Residual) = the difference between observed and predicted values  
Achieving the best fit

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape0]]
R Square: A Measure of Explained Variance

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape1]]
The goal of simple regression is to identify a relationship.  
For multiple predictors, the combined relationship is represented by R Square, which indicates the proportion of variance explained by the model.

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape2_shape0]]
Y

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape2_shape1]]
X

[[BLOCK:slide3_shape2_shape2]]
R Squared

[[BLOCK:slide4_shape0]]
Ideal Conditions for Regression

[[BLOCK:slide4_shape1]]
All predictors are orthogonal (independent of each other).

[[BLOCK:slide5_shape0]]
Overstated Variance Explained

[[BLOCK:slide5_shape1]]
Imagine the overlap between Y and Xs as the variance explained (multiple-relationship).  
Adding more predictors (Xs) to Y can lead to a situation where the circle of Y is nearly fully covered.  
R Square = 0.89! Impressive, but misleading!

[[BLOCK:slide6_shape0]]
An Ineffective Model

[[BLOCK:slide6_shape1]]
A student once asked how to improve his grade. I suggested my fifty-variable regression model, which could predict nearly 89% of test performance: study longer, earn more money, buy a reliable car, watch less TV, browse the Web more often, exercise regularly, attend church, pray frequently, go to fewer movies, play fewer video games, cut hair more often, drink more milk and coffee, and so on.  
This overly complex model is impractical and ineffective!

[[BLOCK:slide7_shape0]]
Critique of Stepwise Regression

[[BLOCK:slide7_shape1]]
Lavery et al. (2016): “Traditional methods (e.g., bivariate screening, stepwise selection, forward selection) are flawed. Statistics provided in regression outputs (p-values, parameter estimates, R2, etc.) were designed for testing a single model and are invalid when used repeatedly as in stepwise regression” (p.1).

[[BLOCK:slide8_shape0]]
Stepwise Regression and Big Data

[[BLOCK:slide8_shape1]]
Smith (2018): “Many Big Data researchers assume that a larger number of explanatory variables makes stepwise regression more effective. However, stepwise regression becomes less efficient as the number of potential explanatory variables increases. It does not address the Big Data challenge of excessive explanatory variables. Instead, Big Data amplifies the shortcomings of stepwise regression.”  
https://doi.org/10.1186/s40537-018-0143-6

[[BLOCK:slide8_note]]
Smith, G. 2018. Step away from stepwise. Journal of Big Data, 5: 1-12. https://doi.org/10.1186/s40537-018-0143-6.

[[BLOCK:slide9_shape0]]
Fitness Data Example

[[BLOCK:slide9_shape1]]
In this example, six variables from Fitness data are used to predict weight. The method applied is OLS regression.

[[BLOCK:slide10_shape0]]
Negative Adjusted R Square!

[[BLOCK:slide10_shape1]]
The R Square is 0.199—not bad, as it explains 20% of the weight variance.  
However, when many predictors are included, adjusted R Square is used to account for inflated R Square.  
Here, it is negative! Since R Square is calculated as r * r, it should not be negative. What is causing this?

[[BLOCK:slide11_shape0]]
Understanding Negative R Square

[[BLOCK:slide11_shape1]]
Zero R Square indicates no variance explained.  
If the fitted line is flat (horizontal), it explains no variance.  
If the model performs worse than a horizontal line and contradicts the data pattern, adjusted R Square can be negative.  
This occurs when too many predictors are included, making the model worse than having no model at all.  
Metaphor: Taking the wrong or fake medicine when sick is worse than taking no medicine.

[[BLOCK:slide12_shape0]]
Challenges of OLS Regression

[[BLOCK:slide12_shape1]]
- Excessive assumptions about residuals and predictors  
- Prone to overfitting  
- Model instability when predictors are highly correlated (Multi-collinearity (Multi-collinearity))  
- No unique solution for large datasets  
- Conventional rule of thumb: 1 predictor per 10 observations; OLS regression is infeasible if predictors exceed observations (p > n)  
- Requires a linear model

[[BLOCK:slide13_shape0]]
Introduction to Generalized Regression

[[BLOCK:slide13_shape1]]
Proposed by Friedman (2008)  
Similar to abduction or Inference to the Best Explanation (IBE): avoid fixation on a single solution; consider multiple possibilities.  
There are often many solutions to a problem—explore alternative paths.  
Start with no modeling or zero coefficients.  
Experiment with a series of models. The solution is flexible (adaptable).  
Select the best model based on algorithmic evaluation, not personal judgment.

[[BLOCK:slide14_shape0]]
KISS Principle in Regression

[[BLOCK:slide14_shape1]]
Also referred to as Regularized Regression (Regularized Regression): aims to reduce variance and enhance generalization, thereby avoiding overfitting.  
Also called Penalized Regression: penalizes overly complex models or inflated fitness → Keep It Simple, Stupid (KISS)!

[[BLOCK:slide15_shape0]]
Defining the Loss Function

[[BLOCK:slide15_shape1]]
A Loss function (Loss function) maps an event involving one or more variables to a real number, representing the cost of that event.  
What is the cost of making a mistake for the event? What is lost?  
In statistics, the cost is represented by error or Residual (Residual).

[[BLOCK:slide16_shape0]]
Loss Function: A Metaphor

[[BLOCK:slide16_shape1]]
Imagine a hiker descending a mountain at dusk. He scans the surroundings for possible routes. If a chosen path does not lead to the destination, he abandons it due to high cost in time and energy.  
Through trial and error, poor choices are eliminated, and the hiker moves toward decreasing loss. Ultimately, he selects the most cost-effective route, minimizing loss.

[[BLOCK:slide18_shape0]]
Different Approaches to Regularization and Penalization
